messages.content:
I don't know. However it seems like it would be relevant for the context provided, the answer would be:

Node.js is not a full-stack JavaScript solution, but rather a way to run your JavaScript outside of a web browser. Node.js allows you to run JavaScript on the server-side by leveraging the Google V8 JavaScript engine to run scripts on a runtime environment.

Luckily	I	wrote	a	free	ebook	that	explains	all	those	topics,	and	it's	called	JavaScript
Fundamentals.	It's	the	most	compact	resource	you'll	find	to	learn	all	of	this.
You	can	find	the	ebook	at	the	bottom	of	this	page:	https://flaviocopes.com/javascript/.
How	much	JavaScript	do	you	need	to	know	to	use	Node?
16

A	brief	history	of	Node
A	look	back	on	the	history	of	Node.js	from	2009	to	today
Believe	it	or	not,	Node.js	is	just	9	years	old.
In	comparison,	JavaScript	is	23	years	old	and	the	web	as	we	know	it	(after	the	introduction	of
Mosaic)	is	25	years	old.
9	years	is	such	a	little	amount	of	time	for	a	technology,	but	Node.js	seems	to	have	been
around	forever.
I've	had	the	pleasure	to	work	with	Node	since	the	early	days	when	it	was	just	2	years	old,	and
despite	the	little	information	available,	you	could	already	feel	it	was	a	huge	thing.
In	this	post,	I	want	to	draw	the	big	picture	of	Node	in	its	history,	to	put	things	in	perspective.

2009
2010
2011

2013

2015

JavaScript	is	a	programming	language	that	was	created	at	Netscape	as	a	scripting	tool	to
manipulate	web	pages	inside	their	browser,	Netscape	Navigator.
Part	of	the	business	model	of	Netscape	was	to	sell	Web	Servers,	which	included	an

The	Node.js	Handbook
The	Node	Handbook	follows	the	80/20	rule:	learn	in	20%	of	the	time	the	80%	of	a	topic.
I	find	this	approach	gives	a	well-rounded	overview.	This	book	does	not	try	to	cover	everything
under	the	sun	related	to	Node.	If	you	think	some	specific	topic	should	be	included,	tell	me.
You	can	reach	me	on	Twitter	@flaviocopes.
I	hope	the	contents	of	this	book	will	help	you	achieve	what	you	want:	learn	the	basics
Node.js.
This	book	is	written	by	Flavio.	I	publish	web	development	tutorials	every	day	on	my	website
flaviocopes.com.
Enjoy!

messages.content:
Based on the provided context:

Node.js is a runtime environment for JavaScript that runs on the server. It's built on top of the Google Chrome V8 JavaScript engine, and it's mainly used to create web servers - but it's not limited to that. Node.js is open source, cross-platform, and since its introduction in 2009, it got hugely popular and now plays a significant role in the web development scene.

Some of the best features of Node.js include:

- Fast execution
- Simple and lightweight
- Runs on JavaScript
- Uses the V8 JavaScript engine
- Asynchronous platform that doesn't block threads
- A huge number of libraries available through the npm (Node Package Manager)

Node.js allows developers to write server-side JavaScript applications, and its popularity is indicated by the large number of GitHub stars, with over 46,000 stars.

An example of a simple Node.js application is a web server, which can be created using the following code:
```javascript
const http = require('http')
const hostname = '127.0.0.1'
const port = 3000
const server = http.createServer((req, res) => {
  res.statusCode = 200
  res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain')
  res.end('Hello World\n')
})
server.listen(port, hostname, () => {
  console.log(`Server running at http://${hostname}:${port}/`)
})
```

documents.pageContent:
What	changes	is	the	ecosystem.
In	the	browser,	most	of	the	time	what	you	are	doing	is	interacting	with	the	DOM,	or	other	Web
Platform	APIs	like	Cookies.	Those	do	not	exist	in	Node,	of	course.	You	don't	have	the
	document	,		window		and	all	the	other	objects	that	are	provided	by	the	browser.
And	in	the	browser,	we	don't	have	all	the	nice	APIs	that	Node.js	provides	through	its	modules,
like	the	filesystem	access	functionality.
Another	big	difference	is	that	in	Node.js	you	control	the	environment.	Unless	you	are	building
an	open	source	application	that	anyone	can	deploy	anywhere,	you	know	which	version	of
Node	you	will	run	the	application	on.	Compared	to	the	browser	environment,	where	you	don't
get	the	luxury	to	choose	what	browser	your	visitors	will	use,	this	is	very	convenient.
This	means	that	you	can	write	all	the	modern	ES6-7-8-9	JavaScript	that	your	Node	version
supports.
Since	JavaScript	moves	so	fast,	but	browsers	can	be	a	bit	slow	and	users	a	bit	slow	to

Introduction	to	Node
This	post	is	a	getting	started	guide	to	Node.js,	the	server-side	JavaScript
runtime	environment.	Node.js	is	built	on	top	of	the	Google	Chrome	V8
JavaScript	engine,	and	it's	mainly	used	to	create	web	servers	-	but	it's	not
limited	to	that
Overview
The	best	features	of	Node.js
Fast
Simple
JavaScript
V8
Asynchronous	platform
A	huge	number	of	libraries
An	example	Node.js	application
Node.js	frameworks	and	tools
Overview
Node.js	is	a	runtime	environment	for	JavaScript	that	runs	on	the	server.
Node.js	is	open	source,	cross-platform,	and	since	its	introduction	in	2009,	it	got	hugely	popular
and	now	plays	a	significant	role	in	the	web	development	scene.	If	GitHub	stars	are	one
popularity	indication	factor,	having	46000+	stars	means	being	very	popular.
Introduction	to	Node
6

When	Node.js	needs	to	perform	an	I/O	operation,	like	reading	from	the	network,	access	a
database	or	the	filesystem,	instead	of	blocking	the	thread	Node.js	will	simply	resume	the
operations	when	the	response	comes	back,	instead	of	wasting	CPU	cycles	waiting.
A	huge	number	of	libraries
	npm		with	its	simple	structure	helped	the	ecosystem	of	node.js	proliferate	and	now	the	npm
registry	hosts	almost	500.000	open	source	packages	you	can	freely	use.
An	example	Node.js	application
The	most	common	example	Hello	World	of	Node.js	is	a	web	server:
const	http	=	require('http')
const	hostname	=	'127.0.0.1'
const	port	=	3000
const	server	=	http.createServer((req,	res)	=>	{
		res.statusCode	=	200
		res.setHeader('Content-Type',	'text/plain')
		res.end('Hello	World\n')
})
server.listen(port,	hostname,	()	=>	{
		console.log(`Server	running	at	http://${hostname}:${port}/`)
})
To	run	this	snippet,	save	it	as	a		server.js		file	and	run		node	server.js		in	your	terminal.

messages.content:
Based on the provided context:

Callbacks are a fundamental part of Node.js. A callback function is a function passed as an argument to another function, which is then invoked inside the other function. In the context of Node.js, callbacks are used to handle asynchronous operations, such as I/O operations (like reading or writing files, network requests, etc.).

When an operation is complete, Node.js executes a specific function, which is the callback function. The callback function takes a single argument, which is the result of the operation.

In Node.js, callbacks are used extensively for handling asynchronous operations, such as:

- Reading and writing files
- Making network requests
- Database operations
- Timers

Callbacks are also used to interact with the Event Loop, which is a mechanism that Node.js uses to execute functions asynchronously.

Node.js provides two main types of callbacks:

- Callback functions
- Async functions (such as `setTimeout` and `setImmediate`)

For example, the following code uses a callback to read a file:
```javascript
const fs = require('fs')

fs.readFile('file.txt', (err, data) => {
  if (err) {
    console.error(err)
  } else {
    console.log(data)
  }
})
```
In this example, the `readFile` function takes a callback as an argument. When the operation is complete, the callback function is executed with the result of the operation.

documents.pageContent:
fundamental	part	of	Node.js:
Asynchronous	programming	and	callbacks
Timers
Promises
Async	and	Await
Closures
The	Event	Loop
How	much	JavaScript	do	you	need	to	know	to	use	Node?
15

setImmediate
The	Node.js	setImmediate	function	interacts	with	the	event	loop	in	a	special
way
When	you	want	to	execute	some	piece	of	code	asynchronously,	but	as	soon	as	possible,	one
option	is	to	use	the		setImmediate()		function	provided	by	Node.js:
setImmediate(()	=>	{
		//run	something
})
Any	function	passed	as	the	setImmediate()	argument	is	a	callback	that's	executed	in	the	next
iteration	of	the	event	loop.
How	is		setImmediate()		different	from		setTimeout(()	=>	{},	0)		(passing	a	0ms	timeout),	and
from		process.nextTick()	?
A	function	passed	to		process.nextTick()		is	going	to	be	executed	on	the	current	iteration	of	the
event	loop,	after	the	current	operation	ends.	This	means	it	will	always	execute	before
	setTimeout		and		setImmediate	.
A		setTimeout()		callback	with	a	0ms	delay	is	very	similar	to		setImmediate()	.	The	execution
order	will	depend	on	various	factors,	but	they	will	be	both	run	in	the	next	iteration	of	the	event
loop.
setImmediate
95

with	a	synchronous	programming	model?
The	answer	was	in	its	environment.	The	browser	provides	a	way	to	do	it	by	providing	a	set	of
APIs	that	can	handle	this	kind	of	functionality.
More	recently,	Node.js	introduced	a	non-blocking	I/O	environment	to	extend	this	concept	to	file
access,	network	calls	and	so	on.
Callbacks
Callbacks
101

• Lead, CodeChef VIT Chennai Chapter: Organized flagship hackathons, technical workshops, and hosted Abdul Bari, driving community 
engagement and technical learning. 
• 100% Attendance Award, University Day 2025: Recognized for maintaining perfect attendance throughout the academic year 2024–2025.

and collaboration skills.                                                                                                                                                                                                                     
 
SKILLS 
Languages: Java, C++, C, Python, JavaScript, TypeScript, R, SQL, HTML, CSS 
Technologies/Frameworks: React.js, Next.js, Node.js, Express.js, Django, FastAPI , TensorFlow, Pandas, NumPy 
Databases: MongoDB, PostgreSQL, MySQL, Firebase, Qdrant, ChromaDB 
Cloud: AWS

Software Developer Intern                                                                                                                                                        Visakhapatnam, India 
• Researched, deployed, and optimized diverse open source LLMs (Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek) on NVIDIA AGX Xavier, benchmarking inference 
performance and integrating Ollama, Docker, Continue, Cline to establish a scalable, cost free local agentic coding platform. 
• Developed an enterprise grade code analysis platform integrating SonarQube, OWASP Dependency Check, and LLMs to automate static 
application security testing (SAST), software composition analysis (SCA), and AI assisted code remediation across all major programming 
languages.
